entities.name: guṇa
entities.cite: A guna here is the dependent member — say, a constituent inside a compound or a bare stem inside a larger unit

entities.name: kārya
entities.cite: grammatical operations do not attach to it directly in that dependent condition

entities.name: artha
entities.cite: the function or value of the element within the whole

axioms.name: paratantra
axioms.cite: Uttered, it is paratantra, not standing on its own

axioms.name: na_yujyate
axioms.cite: grammatical operations do not attach to it directly in that dependent condition

axioms.name: parikalpyate
axioms.cite: the relation of karya to item is parikalpyate, construed, through the artha, the function or value of the element within the whole

claims.name: na_yujyate
claims.cite: the subordinate element is not joined to the operations

claims.name: parikalpyate
claims.cite: the relation of the operations is construed with what those elements mean

rejected_readings.label: quality_not_element
rejected_readings.rendering: Qualities, being dependent on substances, do not undergo grammatical operations directly.
rejected_readings.why: Shifts guna from a grammatical category (constituent, stem in compound) to a metaphysical one (quality-substance relation). The sort error—guna as property rather than linguistic item—breaks the verse's argument about how grammar works with subordinate linguistic elements.

rejected_readings.label: direct_attachment
rejected_readings.rendering: Grammatical operations directly apply to dependent elements without semantic mediation.
rejected_readings.why: Contradicts the verse's core claim by asserting that operations do join dependent elements directly. This ignores the parikalpyate (construing) that the verse establishes—the connection is preserved, not eliminated, but redirected through artha (meaning).

notes: The verse captures a deep principle of Pāṇinian grammar: dependent linguistic elements (constituents, stems in compounds) do not undergo rules directly in their surface form, but rules connect to them through their abstract semantic value or function. The sort 'property' for artha is workable but strained—could also fit as 'cognition' or 'manifestation'—but captures it as a value or attribute of the linguistic item. The contested note flags a metaphysical reading (qualities dependent on substances) which is deliberately excluded to preserve the grammatical focus.
